title: Roast Potatoes
subtitle: Par-boiled and shaken for crisp edges, cooked in duck fat
excerpt: The ideal roast potato has a shatteringly crisp shell and a floury, steam-soft interior. Three things guarantee this: a floury potato variety (Maris Piper or King Edward), par-boiling and shaking the drained potatoes in the pan to rough up the surface, and very hot fat. Duck fat gives the best flavour; goose fat is equivalent; vegetable oil works well.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: potatoes roasted in dripping. Delia Smith, Complete Cookery Course (1978): par-boiling and roughing up the surface. Traditional British Sunday roast practice.

content.text: A floury potato is the starting point. Maris Piper and King Edward are the two varieties consistently recommended for roasting: their high dry-matter content means they become fluffy inside when par-boiled and develop a thick, starchy shell on the outside that absorbs the hot fat and crisps properly. Waxy varieties (Charlotte, Ratte, Jersey Royal) are excellent boiled but poor roasted; they stay firm rather than forming a crust.

is the detail that separates a good roast potato from a great one. Returning the drained par-boiled potatoes to the empty saucepan and shaking them hard roughens the surface into a furry, starchy texture. That surface soaks up hot fat and crisps dramatically in the oven.

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: Maris Piper or King Edward, approx. 1.2 kg, peeled and cut into large chunks

items.name: Duck fat
items.prepNote: or goose fat, beef dripping, or vegetable oil

items.name: Rosemary
items.prepNote: optional, added in the final 10 minutes

content.text: A large roasting pan with room for the potato chunks to sit in a single layer without touching is important. Crowded potatoes steam rather than roast.

Put the potato chunks into a large saucepan with cold water and 1 tsp of salt. Bring to the boil and cook for 8 to 10 minutes until the outside of the chunk is just beginning to give when pressed with a knife tip but the centre is still firm. Drain in a colander.
Return the drained potatoes to the empty hot saucepan. Lid on. Shake the pan hard 10 to 15 times until the potato surfaces are rough and fluffy. The chunks should look slightly ragged, not smooth.

Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan). Put {{duck-fat}} of duck fat in the roasting pan and heat in the oven for 10 minutes until shimmering. Carefully add the roughed-up potato chunks, turning each one to coat in the fat. They should sizzle on contact. Spread in a single layer. Roast for 50 to 60 minutes, turning once at 30 minutes, until deep golden-brown all over. Season with {{sea-salt-flakes}} before serving.

rows.fix: Ensure the fat is smoking before adding potatoes. Space them with gaps between each piece. If steaming, raise the oven temperature by 10°C.
rows.cause: Fat not hot enough, potatoes overcrowded, or too much steam trapped
rows.symptom: Potatoes not crisping, remaining pale and soft

rows.fix: Do not force them. After 30 minutes they will release naturally when a crust forms. Add more fat if the pan looks dry.
rows.cause: Insufficient fat or potatoes not yet ready to release

rows.fix: Use a floury variety. Par-boil until the outer layer is soft before shaking.
rows.cause: Waxy variety used, or par-boil was too short

content.text: Roast potatoes are the most contested dish on the British Sunday table. Families have strong opinions about fat choice, whether to par-boil or parboil-and-freeze, whether rosemary belongs in the pan, and whether semolina should be added to the shaking step (it can; it makes the shell thicker). The duck fat versus dripping versus lard debate is largely a matter of availability and preference: all produce excellent results. What is not debatable is the shaking step and the hot fat. Without both, you have baked potatoes.
